examples.name: single_column
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row in addition to data rows, yielding 4 Blocks ("Col1",
"R1", "R2", "R3") instead of native's 3 (header skipped). Spec
asserts native (skip-header) behaviour.

examples.name: three_columns_two_rows
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi
okf_commaseparatedvalues defaults to one-TextUnit-per-row,
extracting only column 1 (1-based, via sourceColumns="1") and
INCLUDING the header row. Bridge yields 3 Blocks ("name",
"Widget", "Gadget") instead of native's 6 Blocks (every cell
across both data rows). Spec asserts native (cell-per-Block,
skip-header) behaviour.

examples.name: upstream_csv_test1
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — same as
three_columns_two_rows: Okapi yields one Block per row from
column 1 only (4 Blocks: header + 3 data rows). Native yields
one Block per cell across all 7 columns × 3 data rows = 21
Blocks. Spec asserts native behaviour.

examples.name: minimal_layer_wrap
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row, yielding 2 Blocks ("name", "Widget") versus
native's 1 ("Widget", header skipped). Both wrap the document
in StartDocument/EndDocument event pairs; they disagree on
whether the header text is translatable. Spec asserts native
(skip-header) behaviour.

examples.name: single_cell
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row even when no data rows follow, yielding 1 Block
("Col1") versus native's 0 (header skipped, no data rows). Both
wrap the document in document-start/end events; they disagree
on whether a lone header line is translatable. Spec asserts
native behaviour.

examples.name: newline_inside_quoted_field
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row and only column 1 by default; bridge yields 2
Blocks ("Col1", "has\nnewline") versus native's 2 ("has\nnewline",
"normal"). Both honor the RFC 4180 §2.6 contract that the LF
inside the quoted field is part of the value; they disagree
on column selection. Spec asserts native behaviour.

examples.name: blank_row_between_data
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row and only column 1 by default, yielding 3 Blocks
("Col1", "R1C1", "R3C1") versus native's 4 (every non-empty
cell across both data rows). Spec asserts native behaviour.

examples.name: empty_fields_in_row
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts only
column 1 (sourceColumns default), so the empty col-1 cell in
row 2 yields no Block; bridge yields 2 Blocks ("Col1", "R1C1")
versus native's 3 ("R1C1", "R1C3", "R2C2"). Both honor the
empty-cell-skipped contract; they disagree on which columns
are translatable. Spec asserts native (all-columns) behaviour.

examples.name: upstream_csv_test2
quoted_field_with_comma
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row and only column 1 by default, yielding 2 Blocks
("Col1", "has,comma") versus native's 2 cells from the data
row ("has,comma", "normal"). Both honor the RFC 4180 §2.5
contract that the embedded comma is part of the value (not a
delimiter); they disagree on which columns are translatable.
Spec asserts native (all-columns) behaviour.

examples.name: quoted_field_with_doubled_quote
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi yields 2
Blocks ("Col1", `says "hi"`) — header + column 1 of row 1
versus native's 2 ("says \"hi\"", "normal") — both cells of
row 1. Both honor the RFC 4180 §2.7 doubled-quote decoding
contract; they disagree on column selection. Spec asserts
native (all-columns, skip-header) behaviour.

examples.name: cjk_in_value_column
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row + only column 1, yielding 3 Blocks ("key",
"greeting", "farewell"). The CJK text in column 2 never
surfaces. Native extracts every cell → 4 Blocks including
"你好世界" and "さようなら". Both honor UTF-8 verbatim
preservation; they disagree on column selection. Spec asserts
native (all-columns) behaviour.

examples.name: utf8_bom_stripped
examples.detail:
bridge default-config divergence (#530) — Okapi extracts the
header row + only column 1, yielding 2 Blocks ("Col1",
"R1C1"). Native skips the header and extracts both data cells
→ 2 Blocks ("R1C1", "R1C2"). Both correctly strip the UTF-8
BOM from the first cell name; they disagree on column
selection / header handling. Spec asserts native behaviour.

examples.name: backslash_brief
examples.detail:
bridge produces leading space — Okapi DoxygenFilter preserves
the whitespace after the `\brief` command marker that the
native reader trims. Spec asserts native (trimmed) behavior.

examples.name: orphaned_end_tag
examples.detail:
bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
treats the orphaned `</summary>` end tag as a Block boundary,
splitting the comment text into two Blocks where native folds
it into one (HTML inline markup pass-through). Both
legitimate; spec asserts native (single-block) behavior.

examples.name: image_between_prose
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi DoxygenFilter splits the surrounding prose
into two Blocks instead of folding it into one and may extract
the `\image` argument despite the spec contract that `\image`
arguments (filenames, rendering hints) are non-translatable.
Native correctly skips. Tracked separately as a bridge
image-command-handling bug.

examples.name: multi_line_block
examples.detail: bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

examples.name: javadoc_style_h
examples.detail: bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

examples.name: qt_style_h
examples.detail:
bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts 3 Blocks from the qt-style header where native folds
adjacent comment runs into 2. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native (folded) behavior.

examples.name: return_with_description
examples.detail:
bridge produces leading space — Okapi DoxygenFilter preserves
the whitespace after the `\return` command marker that the
native reader trims. Spec asserts native (trimmed) behavior.

examples.name: sample_h
examples.detail:
bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts 8 Blocks across the sample header where native
extracts 7. The has_block_with_text / no_block_with_text
assertions still match in spirit (no untranslatable content
leaks into bridge output), the divergence is purely in
paragraph segmentation. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native (folded) behavior.

examples.name: note_then_warning
examples.detail:
bridge produces leading space — Okapi DoxygenFilter preserves
the whitespace after the `\note` / `\warning` command markers
that the native reader trims. Spec asserts native (trimmed)
behavior on both blocks.

examples.name: special_commands_h
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi DoxygenFilter extracts 10 Blocks instead of
7 from the special-commands header AND extracts the verbatim
block content ("This text is not translated") as a Block,
despite the spec contract that `\verbatim … \endverbatim` is
non-translatable. Native correctly skips. Same root cause as
`verbatim_block_excluded/verbatim_between_prose`. Tracked
separately as a bridge verbatim-handling bug.

examples.name: class_with_header_then_brief
examples.detail:
bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts the trailing details line ("Some details about the
Test class") as a separate Block, where the spec folds it
into the brief description's surroundings. Both legitimate
interpretations of the comment block; spec asserts native
(single-block) behavior.

examples.name: blank_trailing
examples.detail:
bridge segments at finer granularity — Okapi DoxygenFilter
extracts an empty Block for the bare `///<` marker where the
spec contract (and native reader) treat an empty trailing
comment as no extractable content. Spec asserts native
behavior; the divergence is whether an empty-content marker
should still emit an envelope-only Block.

examples.name: consecutive_lines_merge
examples.detail: bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently

examples.name: verbatim_between_prose
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi DoxygenFilter extracts the verbatim block
content ("not translated") as a translatable Block, despite
the spec contract that `\verbatim … \endverbatim` content is
non-translatable. Native correctly skips. Tracked separately
as a bridge verbatim-handling bug.

examples.name: mixed_unknown_and_parameter
examples.detail:
bridge reifies entity references as inline Codes (per Okapi
DTDFilter design — see feature description), so the raw
`&ent1;` / `%pent1;` tokens are stripped from the source
text and only "=ent1, =pent1" remains. Native keeps the
raw tokens in the source. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native behavior.

examples.name: amp_lt_gt_in_one_value
examples.detail:
bridge reifies the predefined XML entities (`&` `<`
`>`) as inline Codes rather than decoding them inline,
so the resulting source text omits `&` and `<...>`. Native
decodes them to literal characters per the Okapi
DTDFilterTest#testDoubleExtraction contract. Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

examples.name: column_beyond_line_end
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — when the only declared column
starts past the actual line length, the upstream Okapi
FixedWidthColumnsFilter falls back to extracting the
whole line as a single Block ("Short"), producing 1
Block. Native correctly skips the column (start beyond
line length → no extractable text → no Block) and
produces 0 Blocks. Both honor the rune-level extraction
contract for in-range columns; they disagree on the
out-of-range fallback. Spec asserts native (skip the
column) behavior.

examples.name: html_block_between_paragraphs
examples.detail:
bridge default config descends into HTML blocks (extracts
"HTML content" as a third translatable Block) instead of
treating them as opaque Data. Native skips HTML blocks
when no htmlSubfilter is configured. Both legitimate;
spec asserts native behavior. Could be reconciled by
explicitly disabling HTML extraction in the bridge config.

examples.name: indented_between_paragraphs
examples.detail:
bridge segments indented code blocks at finer granularity
than native — Okapi MarkdownFilter surfaces the indented
code block as a third Block (visible to the spec) rather
than skipping it as Data. Native skips per
translateCodeBlocks=false default. Both legitimate;
spec asserts native behavior.

examples.name: autolink_in_text
examples.detail:
bridge default config strips autolink URLs from the source
text (treats `<url>` as inline code with no translatable
content), yielding "Visit  now". Native keeps the URL as
part of the source text per CommonMark autolink semantics.
Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

examples.name: bullet_list
examples.detail:
bridge bug — same soft-break handling as emphasis_across_soft_break.
Inside a list item, Okapi MarkdownFilter preserves the literal `\n`
("First\nelement") instead of collapsing it to a space. Native
collapses per CommonMark. Tracked separately as a bridge soft-break
handling bug.

testCases.skipReason: exercises okapi's internal Document/Statements statement-iterator API (verbatim re-serialization of every parsed statement). The native reader has no public statement-stream type; it parses to the content model and reconstructs source via the SkeletonStore, so there is no equivalent surface to assert against.

same internal Document/Statements API, run over every fixture; the native reader has no statement-iterator surface, but the equivalent verbatim re-serialization (byte-exact file reconstruction via the SkeletonStore) is covered by TestSkeletonStore_ByteExact_SimpleFile.

this single okapi test asserts ExtractHardReturnsAsText=false hard-return unit splitting over SEVEN fixtures: 987, 990-marker, 990-pgf-num-format-1, 990-pgf-num-format-2, 990-text-line (all NOW correctly extracted by the native reader — the hard-return-as-new-TextUnit split #558 is implemented across the Para, PgfNumFormat, PgfCatalog, Marker and TextLine paths and is verified in isolation by readFixtureCommonCoded probes matching okapi exactly) AND 990-ref-format-1 / 990-ref-format-2 with setExtractReferenceFormats(true). The two ref-format fixtures require okapi's XRef reference-format REFERENT extraction (inline <XRef>…<XRefEnd> resolving an XRefFormat <XRefDef> into a referent TextUnit whose FrameMaker building blocks become per-block codes), a distinct sub-feature not yet modeled. Because the test bundles all seven fixtures into one assertion, it cannot pass until the XRef ref-format referent chain is implemented; the hard-return split itself (the test's titular behaviour) IS implemented. Same XRef sub-feature blocks referenceFormatsConditionallyExtracted.

asserts okapi's XRefFormats reference-format extraction (XRefDef → XRef building blocks rendered as referent codes) gated by ExtractReferenceFormats over 938-1/938-2/1052.mif. The native reader implements body-page scoping (the 938 fixtures' "Para 1./2./3." extract correctly) but does not yet model the XRefDef→referent building-block code chain that produces okapi's "<bb> Refer to..." / "page <bb>" reference-format units; this is a distinct unimplemented sub-feature (XRef reference-format referents), not a body-page-scoping gap. Tracked for follow-up.

the MERGE counterpart of testSlashCodes: it asserts okapi's byte-exact re-encoding of the same VariableDef whose extracted form depends on the okapi-internal Hexadecimal ILC code-LIST ordering described above (testSlashCodes). The writer cannot reproduce okapi's exact byte stream without first reproducing that internal code ordering on extraction, so this shares the same airtight skip reason.

asserts okapi's internal coded-text ordering of Hexadecimal ILC-wrapped building-block codes in a VariableDef: the expected "<zBold><1/><Default Z Font> ‍<3/><2/>" places the \x0b code (#3) AFTER the <$paratext> code (#2). That ordering is the index a code receives in okapi's TextFragment code LIST (assigned during processILC, MIFFilter.java:989-1018 + Hexadecimal.toString ILC_START/END wrapping, Hexadecimal.java:60-67), which is independent of textual appearance order. The native model assigns inline-code ids in appearance order (a code's id reflects where it occurs in the text), so it would render the same VariableDef as "<zBold><1/><Default Z Font> ‍<2/><3/>". This is an okapi-internal code-index/coded-text representation with no faithful behavioural analogue at the coded-byte level; the building blocks themselves ARE modeled as codes (the <$paranum>/<$paratext> tokens and the \x14/\x05 hex literals decode correctly) — only okapi's code-LIST index ordering differs.

exercises okapi's internal Extracts + FontTags collector classes against every fixture. The native reader has no analogue of these standalone helper types; extraction is verified through the public Read() block stream.

the EXTRACTION half of this fixture is fully implemented and verified by TestTabsRepresentedAsCodesAndHardReturnsAsText / TestTabsRepresentedAsCodesAndNewTextualUnitsFormedOnHardReturnsAppearance (both directions of ExtractHardReturnsAsText, with tabs as inline codes #509 and hard-return unit splitting #558). The MERGE/round-trip half is not event-stable on 1188_crlf.mif: the writer's skeleton-store path (findStringPositions in reader.go) assigns one skeleton ref per inline-code run, while the reader now emits one Block per paragraph (the #558 inline-code model), so a paragraph carrying inline tab codes round-trips with a different Block count (orig 3 → re-extract 2). Making the per-run byte-offset skeleton machine paragraph-aware is a contained but separate reader/writer rework that risks the 6 byte-exact skeleton tests; tracked as the remaining round-trip gap rather than asserted falsely.

the EXTRACTION half (ExtractHardReturnsAsText=false hard-return unit splitting across Para/PgfNumFormat/PgfCatalog/Marker/TextLine, #558) is implemented and verified by TestHardReturnsFormNewTransUnits for 987/990-marker/990-pgf-num-format-1/2/990-text-line; round-trip of 1187_crlf.mif is verified event-stable by TestConsequentialEmptyParaLinesMerged. The full 987/990* round-trip is NOT event-stable for fixtures whose paragraphs carry inline codes/markers: the per-inline-code-run skeleton machine (findStringPositions) and the per-paragraph Block emission (#558) disagree on Block count after extract→write→re-extract (e.g. 990-marker orig 218 → re-extract 215). Same per-paragraph skeleton-store rework as above; tracked rather than asserted falsely.

Okapi serialized-skeleton roundtrip variant (setSerializedOutput(true)); native uses its own skeleton store (no serialized-skeleton mode), and the same #558/#509 event-stability gap as mifFiles applies

native MIF roundtrip is event-unstable over the real /mif/ corpus (per-inline-code Block split, #558/#509) and the native reader panics re-reading writer output for 990-marker.mif; full-corpus event-stable roundtrip is a tracked native gap, not yet satisfiable

examples.name: tab_between_strings
examples.detail:

`<Char Tab>` as a literal tab (U+0009) in the source
text; the two flanking `<String>` runs come through
joined with nothing between them. Native decodes the
tab inline per the Adobe MIF Reference. The text
flanking the tab still surfaces on both sides; the
missing `\t` is the divergence captured here.

bridge bug — Okapi PHPContentFilter does not decode the
`\'` escape inside single-quoted strings, yielding
"It\'s a test" instead of "It's a test". Per the PHP
Manual ("Single quoted") only `\'` and `\\` are valid
inside single-quoted strings and both should decode to
their literal character. Native correctly decodes per
spec. Tracked as neokapi/neokapi#498.

examples.name: two_variables
examples.detail:
bridge default config does not enable Okapi's inline-code
finder for the PHP filter, so `$name` / `$app` stay in the
literal source text ("Hello $name, welcome to $app").
Native applies its built-in PHP-variable detector
unconditionally and exposes the variables as inline codes,
yielding "Hello , welcome to ". Both legitimate; spec
asserts native behavior. Could be reconciled by enabling
`inlineCodes.enabled` in the bridge config (the inline
codes block is reachable in the bridge schema only via a
nested `$ref` so the spec doesn't expose it as a flat key
today — same situation as Properties / PO).

examples.name: escaped_newline_and_tab
escaped_quotes
examples.detail:
Escape decoding divergence — the rule list now rides the
bridge (regexRulesJson), so the bridge DOES extract the Block,
but with the escape undecoded: it emits the raw `Hello
\"World\"`. Okapi's useBSlashEscape only fires inside its
STRING-type delimiter scan and on the write/merge path, not
when emitting the extracted source TextUnit (verified
head-to-head against Okapi 1.48.0). Native decodes \\\" to a
literal quote at extraction. An extraction-layer semantic
difference, not the rules-transport limitation.

examples.name: doubled_quotes
examples.detail:
Escape decoding divergence — the rule list rides the bridge
(regexRulesJson) so the bridge extracts both Blocks, but
leaves the doubled quotes literal (`Hello ""World""`). Okapi's
useDoubleCharEscape decodes only inside its STRING-type
delimiter scan / write path, not at extraction; under
CONTENT-type extraction it decodes nothing. Native collapses
"" → " at extraction. An extraction-layer semantic
difference, not the rules-transport limitation.

examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter keeps the raw
`\%` escape sequence in the extracted Block ("Price is 5\%
off."); native decodes it to a literal `%` ("Price is 5%
off."). Both legitimate; spec asserts native (decode
backslash-percent to a literal `%`) behavior.

examples.name: display_math_bracket
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter folds the
display math into a Code on the surrounding TextUnit;
native flushes the paragraph at the math boundary and
yields two Blocks. Spec asserts native segmentation.

examples.name: display_math_dollar
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — same as inline_math_in_paragraph:
Okapi TEXFilter folds the display math into a Code on the
surrounding TextUnit, native flushes the paragraph at the
math boundary. Spec asserts native segmentation.

examples.name: inline_math_in_paragraph
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter keeps the
surrounding paragraph as one translatable TextUnit with
the math span collapsed into an inline Code, while native
flushes the paragraph at the math boundary and yields two
Blocks ("Energy is" and "here."). Both honor the
contract that the math source itself is non-translatable;
they disagree on whether the surrounding text is one
Block or two. Spec asserts native segmentation.

examples.name: verbatim_between_paragraphs
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter does not skip
the verbatim environment in its default configuration: it
concatenates the verbatim content with the following
paragraph, yielding 2 Blocks ("Before.", "verbatim content
hereAfter."). Native skips the entire \begin{verbatim}…
\end{verbatim} as Data, yielding 2 clean Blocks ("Before.",
"After."). Spec asserts native (verbatim is non-translatable
and isolated from surrounding paragraphs) behavior.

examples.name: split_on_blank_line
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter collapses the
soft line break inside a paragraph, yielding "Second
textThird text"; native preserves it as "Second text\nThird
text". Both yield 2 Blocks. Both legitimate; spec asserts
native (preserve source line breaks inside a paragraph)
behavior.

examples.name: section_optional_short
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter does not extract
the section text when an optional [short] argument precedes
the brace argument: the bridge yields only the body
paragraph ("Body text."), dropping "Long Section Title"
entirely. Native correctly skips the optional argument and
extracts the full title. Both yield 1 Block from the
surrounding body; native yields 2 total Blocks (heading +
body), bridge yields 1. Spec asserts native behavior.

examples.name: section_variants
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter's default
configuration extracts \section/\subsection sections but
omits \subsubsection and \chapter from the paragraph-text
command list, so the bridge yields 3 Blocks ("Section",
"Subsection", "Chapter") while native yields 4 ("Section",
"Subsection", "Subsubsection", "Chapter"). Spec asserts
native (full LaTeX2e sectioning hierarchy is translatable)
behavior.

examples.name: dollar_and_percent
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter handles backslash
escapes differently: it drops the `\$` sequence entirely
(yielding "Price is 10" — the dollar sign and the literal
escape both lost) and keeps `\%` as the raw escape sequence
("5\% off"), giving "Price is 10 and 5\% off.". Native
decodes both escapes to their literal characters, giving
"Price is $10 and 5% off.". Spec asserts native (decode
backslash-escapes to literal characters) behavior.

examples.name: tilde_non_breaking_space
examples.detail:
bridge feature difference — Okapi TEXFilter drops the `~`
(LaTeX non-breaking space) without substitution, yielding
"Wordone is here." (no separating space). Native substitutes
a regular space, yielding "Word one is here.". Spec asserts
native (decode ~ to a regular space in extracted text)
behavior.

     an en→fr two-form numerus message), while the native
     reader emits a single Block carrying both forms in
     numerusform:N properties. Both representations are
     legitimate; the divergence is in the part-stream shape.
  2. Okapi's default code-finder rules (regex from the
     Default Inline Code Pattern) strip `%n` from the source
     text yielding " file(s)" instead of "%n file(s)". The
     native reader treats `%n` as literal text under the
     default config. Both legitimate.
Spec asserts the native single-Block / literal-source view.

examples.name: two_cues_in_sequence
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter defaults to mergeCaptions=true and
merges the two cues into a single block because the first cue
text lacks sentence-terminating punctuation. Native emits one
Block per cue per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

examples.name: chapter_numbers
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter defaults to mergeCaptions=true and
joins intra-cue lines with spaces, so the two chapter cues
collapse to a single block whose text has spaces where newlines
should be. Native emits one Block per cue with line endings
preserved per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

examples.name: align_and_line
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter defaults to mergeCaptions=true and
joins intra-cue lines with spaces, so the two cues with
align/line settings collapse to a single block. Native emits
one Block per cue with line endings preserved per spec
semantics. Tracked separately.

examples.name: empty_then_text
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines, so the non-empty cue's text
reads with a space where the original "\n" should be. Native
preserves line endings per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

examples.name: quoted_text
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines, so quoted multi-line cues
read with a space where the original newline should be.
Native preserves line endings per spec semantics. Tracked
separately.

examples.name: voice_spans
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines. Markup passes through but
"\n" inside <v> spans collapses to a space. Native preserves
line endings per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

examples.name: three_line_cue
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
("Line one Line two Line three.") rather than preserving the
original newlines. Native keeps literal "\n" between lines per
spec semantics. Tracked separately.

examples.name: two_line_cues_with_punctuation
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi VTTFilter joins intra-cue lines with spaces
rather than preserving newlines, so each block's text reads
"Thanks everyone for joining us today." instead of carrying
a literal "\n" between lines. Native preserves line endings
per spec semantics. Tracked separately.

examples.name: scalar_anchor_alias
examples.detail:
bridge segments anchor/alias references at coarser
granularity than native — Okapi YmlFilter exposes the
anchored value once (1 block) instead of emitting at
each use site. Native walks the alias target node directly
(yaml.v3 Node.Alias) and yields one block per occurrence.
Both legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

examples.name: flow_strings_only
examples.detail:
bridge segments flow-style mappings at finer granularity
than native — Okapi YmlFilter extracts both the string and
the numeric scalar (yielding 2 blocks with "John Smith" and
"33"). Native skips non-string scalars under default config
and yields 1 block. Both legitimate; spec asserts native
behavior.

examples.name: folded_block_joins
examples.detail:
bridge bug — folded (`>`) scalar should join continuation
lines with a space and keep the trailing newline ("Joined
text\n"). Bridge yields "Joined text" with no trailing
newline. Native matches YAML 1.2 folded-scalar semantics.
Tracked separately.

examples.name: literal_block
examples.detail:
bridge bug — Okapi YmlFilter loses newlines in literal
(`|`) block scalars, joining "Line 1\nLine 2\n" into
"Line 1Line 2". Native preserves newlines per YAML 1.2
literal-scalar semantics. Tracked separately as a bridge
multiline-block-scalar handling bug.

examples.name: literal_strip_chomp
examples.detail:
bridge bug — same multiline-block-scalar newline loss as
literal_block. Bridge yields "This is avery long line.";
native preserves the embedded newline ("This is a\nvery
long line.") per YAML 1.2 strip-chomp semantics. Tracked
separately.

examples.name: mustache_placeholder
examples.detail:
bridge default config treats `{{count}}` as inline code
(Okapi's default code-finder rules tag and strip it from
the source text), yielding "My  Rails Website". Native
passes mustache placeholders through as plain text. Both
legitimate; spec asserts native behavior.

examples.detail: bridge != native (bytewise) — both pass spec assertions independently
